summary: Yash is a highly organised, deal-savvy final-year computer science student who approaches food with the same systematic thinking he applies to his tech studies. He presents himself as someone who has mastered the student food game - claiming a perfect 50/50 split between cooking and ordering, maintaining a substantial £150-200 weekly food budget, and strategically gaming multiple subscription services he got for free. His communication style is precise and quantified, often breaking down his habits into percentages and systems. However, beneath this controlled exterior lies a more chaotic reality. His diary reveals frequent breakfast skipping, impulsive social eating, and decision-making driven more by convenience and promotions than the balanced approach he describes. He's genuinely enthusiastic about cooking from scratch when he has time, follows Indian food creators for recipe inspiration, and takes his nutrition seriously (tracking macros, high-protein focus). Yet he's also completely comfortable with the delivery ecosystem, viewing it as a practical tool rather than a luxury. His relationship with food is fundamentally transactional - he's constantly calculating deals, comparing apps, and optimising for value, treating the entire food delivery landscape like a system to be hacked.
personality_notes: Systematic, precise communicator who quantifies everything. Confident and articulate but tends to present an idealised version of his habits. Shows genuine enthusiasm when discussing deals and optimisation strategies.
food_relationship: Food is a system to be optimised - he balances cooking from scratch with strategic use of delivery apps, always seeking the best deals and maximum convenience.

key_moments.description: Explains his exact 50/50 cooking vs ordering split with mathematical precision
key_moments.significance: Reveals his systematic approach to food but also suggests some social desirability bias in how he frames his habits

key_moments.description: Details his £150-200 weekly food budget without hesitation
key_moments.significance: Shows he has significant disposable income for a student and isn't particularly price-sensitive despite deal-seeking behavior

key_moments.description: Reveals he got both major subscriptions for free through promotions
key_moments.significance: Demonstrates his skill at gaming the system and explains his positive subscription attitudes

what_not_said: Never mentioned financial stress or budgeting concerns despite being a student
Didn't discuss any negative experiences with delivery apps
Avoided talking about unhealthy eating patterns or guilt around food choices
initial_hypotheses: His systematic approach to food reflects his computer science background and personality
High disposable income allows him to be deal-seeking rather than truly price-sensitive
Free subscriptions have created artificially positive attitudes toward delivery services
His quantified self-description may not match actual behavior patterns
questions_for_coding: How do students with high disposable income use delivery differently?
What role do free/promotional subscriptions play in creating loyalty?
How does systematic personality type interact with food delivery usage?
What's the relationship between claimed vs actual cooking frequency?
diary_summary: Actual eating pattern shows frequent breakfast skipping (4/7 days), regular social eating, and only 1 delivery order during the week, suggesting more cooking and less ordering than his claimed 50/50 split.
say_do_gaps: Claims regular breakfast routine but skipped breakfast 4 out of 7 days
Describes 50/50 cooking vs ordering split but diary shows much more cooking (only 1 delivery order in 7 days)
Presents controlled, systematic approach but diary reflections reveal more impulsive, convenience-driven decisions
